coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot enhanced electrodeposited coatings for advanced early-stage corrosion sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Darani, Masoume Kaviani
Rouhani, Shohre
Ranjbar, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: This work explores a novel approach for metal corrosion by integrating carbon dots (CDs) into electrodeposited (ED) coatings. We detail the synthesis and properties of CDs, making them suitable for corrosion detection. Characterization techniques like Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX), Fluorescent Microscopy and Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL). A Design of Experiment (DOE) optimized parameters like CD concentration, curing temperature, and voltage for improved coating performance. The optimized conditions (0.4 wt% M-CDots, 165 °C curing, and 36.2 V) resulted in enhanced adhesion and corrosion resistance. CD-infused coatings demonstrated superior adhesion and mechanical stability compared to controls. Electrochemical tests confirmed a 10 % improvement in corrosion resistance and PL study demonstrated an approximately 60 % enhancement in emission intensity. The adhesion was not affected by introducing carbon dots to the coating. Monitoring the onset of metal corrosion on samples in salt spray cabinets was achieved through the use of a UV source. The photoluminescence of M-CDs offered real-time, non-destructive monitoring of early-stage corrosion through quenching in the presence of exposed metal surfaces. This research presents a significant advancement by integrating CD-based sensing into corrosion-resistant coatings, holding promise for various industries and leading to improved safety, reduced maintenance costs, and extended equipment lifespans. Notably, the biocompatible and disposable nature of CD-based sensors further enhances their practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron corrosion
Phenylenediamine carbon dots
Optical sensor
Iron ion detector

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of M-CDots preparation process.
The dried M-CDots powder after each step (a), and the solutions after each purification step under UV light 366 nm (b).
Characterization of synthesized and purified M-CDots. a) FTIR, b) SEM image, c) zeta sizer, and d) EDX.
The dependence of M-CDots photoluminescence intensity to Fe2+ Concentrations (A1 and A2) and to Fe3+ concentrations (B1 and B2).
Digital photograph of the 20 coatings of DOE under daylight (left) and under UV light (right).
Digital photograph of the cross-cut (a) and pull-off test results under day light and UV light (b and c, respectively) and PL.
Normal probability plots, plot of residuals vs. predicted response, residual plots, and main effect plots for adhesion and log IZI.
Contour plots and surface three-dimensional plot showing the relationship between main parameters and adhesion/log IZI as responses.

Digital photograph of liquid resins of ED and DOE optimized ED/M-CDots under daylight and UV light (A), pull-off adhesion of pure ED coating and DOE optimized ED/M-CDots coating (B), EIS results of ED coating and DOE optimized ED/M-CDots coating (C), and 1 % attenuated PL of ED coating and DOE optimized ED/M-CDots coating (D).
Nyquist plots of ED Coating (a,b,c) and ED/M-CDots Coating (d,e,f,g) during immersion in NaCl solution for 62 days.
Bode modulus and phase plots for both ED and ED/M-CDots coatings (a and b, respectively).
Tofel Plots of the ED and ED/M-CDots coatings after 62 days of immersion in NaCl solution.
Digital photograph of the ED coating and ED/M-CDots coating with three different artificial defects after placing in a salt spray chamber under day and UV light.
Digital photograph of the ED and ED/M-CDots coating with a linear artificial defect under day light and UV source after 48 h of salt spray test.

Fluorescent microscope images of: (a) detached ED/M-CDots coating from the metal surface after placement in the salt spray chamber far from the scratches, and (b) detached ED/M-CDots coating from the metal surface near the scratches after 24 h of placement in the salt spray chamber. Both images are magnified 20 times under 405 nm excitation.

Photoluminescence spectrum of a 1:2 mixture of a 3.5 % NaCl salt solution and a 0.04 % M-CDots aqueous solution measured under excitation at two wavelengths, 340 and 366 nm (a, b, e, f, respectively), both in the absence and presence of an uncovered metal immersed in the solution. Graph of changes in photoluminescence intensity over time (c, d, g, h).
DOE parameters.
Full table for RSM DOE parameters and responses.
analyze of variance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Prediction of optimized responses.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot enhanced electrodeposited coatings for advanced early-stage corrosion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Surface Coating and Corrosion, Institute for Color Science and Technology, P. O. Box: 167654-654, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Surface Coating and Corrosion
Institute for Color Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Surface Coating and Corrosion, Institute for Color Science and Technology, P. O. Box: 167654-654, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Colorant, Institute for Color Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Color Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic Colorant, Institute for Color Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work explores a novel approach for metal corrosion by integrating carbon dots (CDs) into electrodeposited (ED) coatings. We detail the synthesis and properties of CDs, making them suitable for corrosion detection. Characterization techniques like Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX), Fluorescent Microscopy and Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL).

A Design of Experiment (DOE) optimized parameters like CD concentration, curing temperature, and voltage for improved coating performance. The optimized conditions (0.4 wt% M-CDots, 165 °C curing, and 36.2 V) resulted in enhanced adhesion and corrosion resistance.

CD-infused coatings demonstrated superior adhesion and mechanical stability compared to controls. Electrochemical tests confirmed a 10 % improvement in corrosion resistance and PL study demonstrated an approximately 60 % enhancement in emission intensity. The adhesion was not affected by introducing carbon dots to the coating. Monitoring the onset of metal corrosion on samples in salt spray cabinets was achieved through the use of a UV source. The photoluminescence of M-CDs offered real-time, non-destructive monitoring of early-stage corrosion through quenching in the presence of exposed metal surfaces.

This research presents a significant advancement by integrating CD-based sensing into corrosion-resistant coatings, holding promise for various industries and leading to improved safety, reduced maintenance costs, and extended equipment lifespans. Notably, the biocompatible and disposable nature of CD-based sensors further enhances their practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its excellent mechanical properties, superb thermal conductivity, good electrical conductivity, and low cost, carbon steel is favored in many industries such as aviation, marine, construction steel, oil and gas pipeline industries [1]. Nonetheless, the risk of metal corrosion in the presence of humid atmosphere, salt solution, acid, and alkali is high [2]. Metal corrosion not only poses substantial economic costs but also compromises the safety and integrity of various industrial and infrastructure assets [3].

This degradation reduces service life, causes huge economic losses, and poses a great threat to life safety [4]. In the continuing battle against the pervasive threat of metal corrosion, researchers and engineers are continually seeking innovative solutions to detect and mitigate this destructive phenomenon at its earliest stages. There are several methods to detect corrosion, such as weight loss measurement, electrochemical methods, electrical resistance probes, and linear polarization resistance (LPR) [5]. The problem is that these methods require instruments, are time-consuming, and do not fast response. Traditional corrosion detection methods often fall short when it comes to identifying corrosion in its incipient stages, where damage may be imperceptible to the naked eye [6]. In the electrochemical process, using inorganic coating, metal degradation occurs in the interface of metal and coating, As a result, the presence of an iron component can be used as a detector [7].

In recent years, the world of nanotechnology has opened new frontiers for corrosion monitoring and prevention by introducing optical probes [8,9]. These nanoprobes have excellent selectivity, remarkable sensitivity, and easy operation [10,11]. CDots, with their remarkable properties such as biocompatibility, abundance of raw materials in nature, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness, have emerged as a promising candidate for enhancing corrosion-sensing capabilities [12,13]. These tiny, luminescent nanoparticles can revolutionize the way we perceive and combat corrosion. These fluorescent have excellent optical properties such as anti-photobleaching, light stability, no light flicker, and a wide excitation wavelength range [14–16].

In a study, they focused on the synthesis and application of carbon dots (CDs) doped lanthanide coordination polymers (LnCPs) for the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of iron Fe ions (Fe2+ and Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA). The CDs doped LnCPs, named AMP-Tb-Phen@CDs, offer advantages such as facile preparation, effective excitation at a single wavelength, and ratiometric readout for improved accuracy. The study introduced a masking agent (Phen) for selective detection of and Fe3+ and a reducing agent (NH2OH·HCl) for total iron determination when both ions coexist. The fluorescent probe demonstrated high selectivity for Fe2+ and Fe3+, allowing reliable quantitative analysis. The masking agent Phen selectively complexes with Fe2+ in the presence of Fe3+, facilitating accurate quantification. Furthermore, the study extends the sensor's utility to detect AA in the presence of Fe3+, showcasing its versatility for multi-analyte ratio-metric assays [17]. In another study, citric acid and ammonia were used as the carbon precursor and nitrogen dopant, respectively, to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through a simple solvothermal treatment [18]. These CDs, exhibiting bright blue fluorescence, demonstrated a selective and effective detection of Fe3+ over other metal ions. The researchers fabricated CDs-based pattern papers and hydrogels using cellulose and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as matrices. The prepared disposable CDs materials were successfully applied for the detection of iron corrosion through both instrumental and naked-eye testing, providing an easy and visual method to monitor the safety of metal materials.

Doping CDots with heteroatom-like nitrogen results in enhanced fluorescence intensity [19–21]. Xu et al. studied the effect of nitrogen doping content on the fluorescence intensity of CDots [22]. Using three different concentrations of ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source in the synthesis process, they concluded that the CDots quantum yield is proportional to the percentage of nitrogen doping [23–25].

The surface characteristics of CDots make them suitable for polymer inclusion, resulting in hybrid composites with superior optical properties [26]. The polymers act as a stabilizing agent for high-surface energy nanoparticles. Polymer chains tend to diffuse through the surface of nanoparticles to compensate for the starving nature of nanoparticle surface energy [27]. By embedding CDots within ED coatings, can unlock a realm of possibilities for early-stage corrosion detection and protective measures.

Anti-corrosion coatings, especially waterborne anti-corrosion coatings containing less toxic heavy metals and low volatile organic compounds (VOCs), have attracted considerable attention [28]. Epoxy resin has been widely used in the corrosion protection of metal surfaces because of its excellent mechanical properties, improved corrosion resistance, high chemical resistance, and perfect adhesion to metal substrates [29]. Epoxy waterborne ED coating systems are advanced paint and coating technologies that offer various advantages in terms of performance, environmental friendliness, and application efficiency [30]. These coatings are commonly used in industries such as automotive, appliances, and general metal finishing.

ED coatings are a cornerstone of corrosion protection in the automobile industry and home appliances, safeguarding these essential products from the destructive effects of rust and degradation. However, early detection of any potential breaches in this protective layer is critical for timely intervention and extending product lifespan. This research presents a novel approach by incorporating carbon dots into the ED coating for the first time.

In this study, we used citric acid as the carbon source and meta-phenylene diamine as the nitrogen source. Solvothermal synthesis occurred in an autoclave filled with DMF as the solvent. The M-CDots with blue fluorescence embedded in epoxy water-borne ED coating for the first time could sensitively detect iron ions (Fe2+/Fe3+) as the corrosion started. Due to the complexing of M-CDots with iron ions, fluorescence quenching is used as a signal for the detection of failure through the naked eye under a UV source, which provides a simple yet easy visual method for monitoring the safety of coated metal materials.

The synthesis of M-CDots was performed via a solvothermal method using citric acid (CA, 99%-Merck, Germany) as the carbon precursor, 1,3-phenylene diamine (MPD-Sigma-Aldrich, USA) as the nitrogen source, and dimethylformamide (DMF, Supelco, USA) as the solvent according to previous studies (different solvent and optimized temperature according to lab conditions) [31]. This method was chosen because of its ability to produce CDots with well-defined properties and controlled sizes. It should be noted that all the chemicals were used as received with no further purification.
Precursor Preparation: CA and MPD were weighed 1.68 gr and 0.64 gr respectively, and 40 ml DMF was added. The solution was thoroughly mixed to ensure homogeneity.

Solvothermal Reaction: The precursor solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. This high-temperature and high-pressure environment facilitated the formation of CDots.
Cooling and Collection: After the solvothermal reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The resulting solution contained CDots in the DMF.

To isolate and purify the M-CDots, the brown solution after the solvothermal reaction was heated at 80 °C to remove the solvent (see Fig. 1). The dried powder was then solved in dichloromethane (Merck). After agitation, de Ionized (DI) water was added and mixed well. The solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 9000 rpm. The two phases were separated. The CDots in the aqueous phase were dried in an oven at 80 °C for 3 h and used in the subsequent process (Fig. 2
).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: For iron ions detection, 2 mL M-CDots solution in DI water (0.04 wt%) was prepared. Stock solutions of metal salts (FeCl3 and FeCl2, Merck) were dissolved in deionized water. Both solutions were diluted with different concentrations of DI water to obtain a series of Fe2+ and Fe3+ solutions with known concentrations. Upon adding the metal ions solution to the M-CDots solution, the fluorescence spectra of M-CDots at 340 nm excitation wavelength were measured to study the fluorescence response of CDs toward iron ions.

For the preparation of coatings, M-CDots were dispersed in an epoxy waterborne ED coating using ultrasonication. To prepare a sample, 0.4 g of M-CDots were weighed and added to 100 g of ED coating. The mixture was subjected to sonication for 3 min, followed by immersion in an ultrasonic bath for an additional 4 min. This process facilitates the effective dispersion of the M-CDots and ensures the uniform application of the ED coating on the nanoparticles. Ultrasonic treatment enhances the homogeneity and stability of the resulting nanoparticle dispersion. The final solution appeared as a light brown milky fluid.
Prior to coating process, each metal plate surface was prepared by cleaning with acetone, followed by sanding and a final rinse with acetone and ethanol.

For the electrochemical measurements, a defined area of the electrode surface (1 cm × 1 cm) was exposed for measurement using a beeswax mask. The beeswax was applied to the entire plate surface, leaving a precisely 1 cm × 1 cm square uncoated. This ensured only the desired area was exposed to the electrolyte solution during the experiments.

Test coatings were then prepared based on a Design of Experiment (DOE) conducted using Minitab software to optimize the M-CDots concentration in the coating (g/L), applied voltage, and curing temperature. All suggested test runs were prepared, cured under the defined conditions (time, atmosphere, if applicable), and further examined.

For the preparation of coatings, the M-CDots were dispersed in epoxy waterborne ED coating by ultrasound. For example, to prepare a sample and disperse nanoparticles, 0.4 gr of M-CDots was weighed and added to 100 g of ED coating directly. The sample was subjected to probing ultrasound for 3 min, followed by placement in an ultrasonic bath for an additional 4 min. This process facilitates the effective dispersion of M-CDots and ensures the uniform application of the ED coating on the nanoparticles. Ultrasonic treatment was employed to enhance the homogeneity and stability of the resulting nanoparticle dispersion. The final solution was a light brown milky fluid.
For each sample, a metal plate surface was prepared by acetone, cleaned with sandpaper, and washed again with acetone and ethanol.

For the preparation of test coatings, the design of the experiment was carried out to optimize the M-CDots concentration in the coating, voltage, and curing temperature using Minitab. All suggested test runs were prepared, cured, and further examined.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR): The chemical composition and functional groups of the synthesized M-CDots were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). Spectra were collected over a 400-4000 cm−1 range to identify the presence of characteristic vibrational modes.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX): The morphological features and elemental composition of the M-CDots were examined by SEM imaging at various magnifications (Tescan Mira3 LMU, Czechia). EDX analysis was performed simultaneously to determine the elemental composition and distribution of the M-CDots.
Zeta Sizer: To further analyze the size of M-CDots, they were examined by HORIBA SZ-100 (Japan).

Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL): The photoluminescent properties of the M-CDots were investigated using PL spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). Excitation and emission spectra were recorded to assess the optical characteristics, such as emission wavelength and intensity. A 1 % attenuator was used because of the high intensity of Photoluminescence.

Fluorescent Microscopy: The quenched carbon dots were imaged using a Leica DMI6000 B fluorescence microscope system (Leica Microsystems, Germany) with excitation at 405 nm and carbon dots emission at 460 nm.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS): Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were performed to assess the corrosion resistance of the ED coatings. EIS data were collected over a frequency range while applying a small AC voltage to the coated metal substrates (IVIUM, Netherland). The impedance spectra were analyzed to determine the corrosion parameters, such as the charge transfer resistance and corrosion rate.
The changes in the OCP values corresponding to the immersed samples were recorded at different times with respect to saturated Calomel electrode (SCE).

Pull-Off Adhesion Test: The adhesion strength of the ED coatings to the metal substrates was evaluated using a pull-off adhesion test (Defelsko, PosiTest AT, USA). A specified loading force was applied perpendicular to the substrate, and the force required to detach the coating was recorded. The tests were carried out according to ASTM D4541.

Cross-Cut Adhesion Test: Cross-cut adhesion testing (PosiTest, USA) was performed to assess the adhesion and cohesion properties of the coating. A grid pattern was scribed onto the coated surface, and the adhesive tape was applied and removed. The extent of coating removal or damage was examined and reported.
Coating Thickness measurement: the dry-film thickness was measured using an ultrasonic (Defelsko, Positector 200, USA) nondestructive method on metal panels.

Data Analysis: The data obtained from these characterization techniques were analyzed to determine the physical, chemical, and mechanical properties of the M-CDots and ED coatings. Statistical methods and software were used to interpret the results, establish correlations, and optimize the coating parameters.

Metaphenylene (M-CDots) were synthesized and examined in terms of their functional groups, size, morphology, and elemental analysis. The results were consistent with the references and demonstrated well-synthesized M-CDots (Fig. 3 ).

To examine the sensitivity of synthesized M-CDots toward iron ions, stock solutions of M-CDots were treated with various concentrations of iron ions. As a result, the photoluminescence of the M-CDots started quenching by increasing the concentration of iron ions. This may be due to the coordination between iron ions and hydroxyl groups on the surface of M-CDots [32–34]. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , in both cases, increasing iron ion led to decrease in photoluminescence intensity, which makes these M-CDots a suitable probe for iron ion detection.

The diagrams generated from the Design of Experiment (DOE) and the optimization results play a crucial role in guiding the decision-making process and enabling the fine-tuning of factors to achieve desired outcomes. Herein, we specified three parameters, concentration of M-CDots in ED resin, temperature, and electrodeposition voltage. As a Response Surface Methodology, central composite design, 20 runs were suggested (Table 1 ).

For the responses, log IZI extracted from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and adhesion power evaluated by pull-off test were used. These responses were selected because of the significant importance of maintaining the unchanged performance of the coating by adding nanoparticles. It must be ensured that the final coating has high adhesion to the substrate to protect the structure and also maintains coating cohesion for corrosion protection. The ANOVA chart is shown in Table 2 . According to the P-Values, the temperature and voltage terms were significant.

Based on the 20 runs, 20 samples were prepared by varying three parameters (Fig. 5 ). All the 20 cured samples were then photographed under daylight and UV light. In each test, three samples were used to assess accuracy and reproducibility. This practice of using multiple samples enhances the reliability of the experimental results by providing a basis for evaluating the consistency and repeatability of the conducted tests.

After measuring the thickness (varied from 5 to 30 μm), several tests were carried out on the samples, including cross-cut adhesion test, pull-off adhesion test, photoluminescence spectra, and EIS after 1 h immersion of the samples in NaCl solution (3.5 %). The results were extracted and placed in the software for further investigation and optimization (Fig. 6 and Table 2). As shown in Fig. 6, the substrate with removed part of the coatings during examination has no luminescence under a UV source. The dollies after the pull-off test are shown in the bottom, both under daylight and UV 366 nm.

After all measurements were performed, the responses for each test were uploaded to the Minitab software for further analysis. The results are shown in Table 2. Due to the similar results for cross-cut adhesion test for the samples, the pull-off adhesion results were introduced to software as the adhesion power of the coatings.
In the next step, data were transferred to the Minitab software to analyze the influence of each parameter.

Table 3 presents the results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) for the DOE conducted in the study. This statistical analysis assesses the significance of various factors and their interactions on the response variables, namely adhesion strength and electrochemical impedance.

The overall model's F-value of 2.64 for adhesion as a response indicates that the model has a p-value of 0.050. The linear terms, including concentration, temperature, and voltage, collectively exhibit a significant effect on the response variables, as indicated by the low p-value of 0.011. temperature exhibits a lower p-value (0.056), indicating a relatively more significant impact on the response variables and it indicates that the terms in the model have a significant effect on the response [35]. With a p-value of 0.458, the lack-of-fit test suggests that the model fits the data adequately, indicating that the model is appropriate for the observed responses. An examination of the Fit Summary output revealed that the quadratic model is statistically significant for both responses and therefore; it will be used for further analysis.

However, regarding the response of log IZI, only the voltage of the model has a significant effect. The sensitivity of log IZI to voltage alterations suggests a direct correlation with the voltage, emphasizing the importance of controlling and manipulating the voltage parameter in the context of the model.

Factorial plots known as interaction plots of responses are shown in Fig. 7 . These plots visualize the effects of different factors and their interactions on a response variable. As can be seen, changing temperature and voltage have stronger effects on the responses.

The normal probability plots for the residuals and the residuals versus predicted response plots for both responses are presented in Fig. 7. The alignment of residuals along the straight line in Figures indicates a normal distribution of errors for both responses. Plots for residuals versus Fits depict the absence of any discernible pattern. This implies that the model is appropriate, and there is no evidence to suggest a violation of assumptions such as constant variance or independence. The normality of the residuals in the Figures strengthens the validity of the model and supports the reliability of the statistical inferences drawn from the analysis.

Contour plots are especially useful when visualizing the interaction between two factors. Contour plots show how response levels change across a two-dimensional space, where each axis corresponds to a factor (Fig. 8 ). In the contour plots, the stronger green belongs to the area in which the response is higher. These plots serve guides for decisions related to process optimization, quality improvement, and product development. In both sets of plots, the greater influence of the electric deposition voltage is evident. For log IZI, higher values are observed at higher voltages, whereas for adhesive strength, optimal results are achieved within a specific voltage range.

Three-dimensional plots of adhesive strength and corrosion resistance are provided. Both responses exhibit curvature following the fitted second-degree model. These plots visualize the complex relationships between the input factors and responses, highlighting the nonlinear nature of the system. The curvature in the plots reflects the impact of interactions and nonlinear effects of the factors on adhesive strength and corrosion resistance. The fitted second-degree model effectively captures these intricate relationships, allowing for a more comprehensive understanding of the behavior of the system.

Response surface models are mathematical models derived from the DOE data. These models are allied for the optimization of the responses (maximize the response variable). The recommended conditions for achieving the maximum response variable after calculation are shown in Table 4 .

According to the prediction, the sample containing 0.4 wt% M-CDots cured at 165 °C and applied voltage of 36.2 V, must have the maximum pull-off strength and impedance. To investigate the accuracy of the model, this sample was prepared alongside a sample cured under similar conditions but without any carbon nanoparticles (blank ED coating).
The two samples were examined in the previous analysis (Fig. 9 ). The results were not only in accordance with the prediction but also optimized compared to the pure ED coating.

The EIS, pull-off adhesion test, and PL test results demonstrated that CND-infused coatings exhibited superior adhesion strength and mechanical stability compared with reference coatings. This enhancement can be attributed to the unique properties of M-CDots, such as their uniform distribution and strong interfacial interactions.

The anticorrosion behavior of the ED coatings with/without M-CDots nanoparticles was investigated by monitoring the variation in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy plots of the samples with the immersion time of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out with a three-electrode system in a 3.5 wt% NaCl buffer at room temperature (25 °C). The saturated calomel electrodes acted as reference electrodes (3 M KCl), the platinum sheet (2 faces, each 1*1 cm2) acted as a counter-electrode, and the coated steels acted as working electrodes. Before testing, the open circuit potential (OCP) was performed for 30 min to maintain stable states. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out in a scanning frequency range from 10000 Hz to 0.01 Hz at the OCP with 0.01 V sinusoidal signal. The Tafel polarization curves (TPCs) were scanned from cathodic to anodic sites (±200 mVOCP) at a scan rate of 1 mV/s.

Nyquist plots of ED coatings with and without M-CDots nanoparticles at different immersion times in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution is presented in Fig. 10 . At the initial immersion stage (30 min), the Nyquist plots show a large capacitive loop in the high and middle frequency ranges corresponding to the coating response, and a small capacitive loop in the low-frequency range attributed to the response of the electrochemical reaction for the ED/M-CDots coating. Here, these two capacitive loops overlap owing to the weak electrochemical reaction at this duration, thus it is difficult to distinguish the second small capacitive loop at the low-frequency range. Overall, the diameter of the capacitive loop decreases with increasing immersion time. However, with the extension of immersion time, the corrosive medium diffuses continuously along the coating defects and reaches the coating–metal interface, causing coating failure. This trend can be seen in the ED coating with smaller loops.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the low-frequency impedance values of all coatings on steel were maintained at approximately 109 and 1010 Ωc2m at 30 min, which indicated that both ED and ED/M-CDots coatings on steel exhibited the best corrosion resistance at the beginning of service. The maximum phase angle (about 90°) was measured [6]. Probably, due to the cross-linking density of the coating on steel being improved by M-CDots and the functional groups on the surface of M-CDots, which can interact with the epoxy groups of the ED matrix, the initial low-frequency impedance value of the ED/M-CDots coating on steel was higher than that of the pure ED coating on steel.

As can be seen, the low-frequency impedance values of the ED and ED/M-CDots coatings were almost maintained at about 108 Ω cm2 after 7 days of immersion. This result indicates that all coatings effectively protect the metal during short-term service. The slow reduction of impedance after 14 days of immersion indicated that M-CDots could dramatically improve coating corrosion resistance by enhancing coating compactness and providing reliable protection for carbon steel in long-term service. Interestingly, the ED coating on steel impedance values were similar, related to the protection of the corrosion products film on the metal surface. The low-frequency impedance value of coatings on steel less than 106 Ω cm2 after 28 days, implied that the coating had completely lost its ability to protect Q235 steel [36]. Furthermore, the phase plot could observe two maximum values (two-time constants). This phenomenon indicated that it was difficult for the coating on steel to maintain long-term corrosion resistance in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The anti-corrosion performance of the ED/M-CDots coating on steel could be explained from two perspectives. On the one hand, the M-CDots can fill the holes in the coating and enhance coating compactness. On the other hand, the M-CDots could act as a corrosion inhibitor to adsorb on the steel surface to form a protective film, preventing contact between the corrosion media and the Q235 steel substrate [37].

Tafel extrapolation is one of the polarization methods widely used to measure corrosion rates, and it is a faster experimental technique than classical weight-loss estimation [38]. As can be seen in Fig. 12 , the corrosion rate in ED/M-CDots is lower than that in the ED coating.

The lower i cor. (current density is a measure of the rate of metal dissolution due to corrosion) For ED/M-CDots coating implies better corrosion resistance provided by this coating compared to ED Coating. For the ED coating, similar slopes for both anodic and cathodic reactions (0.096 and 0.099 V/dec, respectively) suggest a more balanced corrosion process. In contrast, for the ED/M-CDots coating, the anodic reaction slope (0.089 V/dec) is higher than the cathodic reaction slope (0.077 V/dec), indicating a higher rate of the anodic reaction. This implies that metal dissolution (anodic reaction) occurs at a faster rate compared to the reduction reactions (cathodic reactions). This may be due to the formation of a passive film on the metal surface or the corrosion inhibition role of M-CDots.

The introduction of M-CDots into the coating matrix can enhance corrosion protection efficiency by providing active sites for better adhesion and promoting the formation of a more uniform and protective coating. Moreover, the ability of M-CDots to act as electron reservoirs facilitates efficient charge transfer processes during Tafel polarization experiments, leading to improved electrochemical performance. The synergistic effects of M-CDots in ED coatings contribute to enhanced corrosion resistance, making them valuable additives for achieving superior protective properties in various applications.

To further evaluate the corrosion resistance of the composite coatings in an actual application environment, a salt spray test was performed on the samples with artificial scratches. For the tests, the scratched coating samples were placed in a spray chamber with a continuous salt fog of 5 wt% NaCl at 35 °C.

Salt spray corrosion is one of the most common and destructive corrosion behaviors of metals in harsh environments. Therefore, the neutral salt spray test is a simple and effective technique to evaluate the long-term corrosion resistance of coatings [6]. The test was carried out according to ASTM B117.

Before testing, three different types of defects were artificially made on the surface of the coating; cross scratches, dot defects, and horizontal line scratches. Digital photographs of the ED and ED/M-CDots coating on steel at different times are shown in Fig. 13 . It is worth mentioning the thickness of the coatings was measured to be 9 ± 2 μm. As can be seen, the brown rust formed inside the scratch area on the steel panels. This phenomenon was attributed to the penetration of aggressive ions (H2O, O2, and Cl-) into the coating matrix, causing the metal oxidation reaction [39]. With extending the test time, more corrosion products are accumulated inside the scratches. After 72 h, corrosion media diffusion to the metal surface intensified, leading to further fall off of the ED coatings. Meanwhile, this diffusion starts to be seen by the naked eye in the ED/M-CDots coating. Therefore, the pure ED coating failed to protect the metal surface in a shorter time. This can be described as follows: When the coating is damaged, M-CDots form a shielding film on the Q235 steel surface, inhibiting the diffusion of corrosive media ((H2O, O2, and Cl−) [3]. This result is in agreement with the electrochemical impedance data.

The existence of the voids and more free spaces in the coatings with no nanoparticles caused the corrosive chloride ions to access the interface of the coating/steel to a greater extent and with a deeper penetration, consequently, the corrosion value was heightened.
Moreover, the homogenous distribution of M-CDots through the polymer matrix and the filling of empty voids with these nanoparticles enhanced the resistance to corrosion.

As seen in Fig. 14 , the diffusion of corrosive media before the accumulation of corrosion products is not obvious to the naked eye in daylight. However, placing the sample under UV light reveals the areas where these corrosive media have penetrated to the metal/coating interface and where the corrosion is still in the early stages. This allows for potential prevention before the complete failure of the coating.

To gain a deeper understanding of the corrosion process, fluorescence microscopy was employed. For this purpose, two samples were prepared: one from the coated film containing carbon dots after exposure to the salt spray chamber detached from the far parts from scratch, and the other from the film on the metal surface, after the salt spray test, and detached from near the scratch. The resulting images are presented in Fig. 15 . As can be observed, the darkened areas due to corrosion on the film and around the scratch are clearly visible.

In this image, A, a uniform and bright fluorescence can be seen, indicating that the M-CDots are likely evenly distributed throughout the coating. This suggests the coating is intact and the M-CDots are functional. Image B depicts the coating near scratches, where the fluorescence appears dimmer and less uniform, indicating a stronger negative impact from the salt spray. This difference suggests the scratches might have facilitated deeper corrosive media penetration or increased local stress, leading to more pronounced quenching of the M-CDots in those areas.

To investigate the effect of a salt solution on the photoluminescence of CDots and the quenching of photoluminescence by iron ions, the experimental setup aimed to explore the impact of a salt solution, specifically a 3.5 % NaCl solution, on the photoluminescence behavior of M-CDots and the quenching effect induced by iron ions (Fe3+). The prepared mixture consisted of a 1:2 ratio of the NaCl solution and a 0.04 % aqueous solution of M-CDots. Photoluminescence spectra were acquired with excitations at 340 and 366 nm (Fig. 16 ).

The results showed that contrary to expectations, the photoluminescence of M-CDots did not diminish in the presence of chloride ions (Cl−) over time. This observation suggests that the Cl− ions did not induce quenching of the photoluminescence of M-CDots, even over time.

In a parallel experiment, an uncovered metal piece was introduced into the mixture under the same conditions. As time progressed, the photoluminescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded at different intervals. In this case, the observed reduction in photoluminescence intensity can be attributed to the generation of iron ions from the metal and the subsequent interaction between these ions and the functional groups on the surface of M-CDots.

The scientific rationale behind this phenomenon lies in the interaction between iron ions and the M-CDots. The presence of iron ions decreased the intensity of the photoluminescence spectrum. This reduction can be explained by the formation of complexes or interactions between the iron ions and functional groups on the surface of the M-CDots, leading to a quenching effect. The increase in metal ions over time further intensified this quenching, indicating a dynamic process in which the interaction between iron ions and M-CDots evolves with time, thereby affecting the photoluminescence behavior of the system.

In conclusion, this comprehensive study has illuminated the transformative potential of (M-CDots-embedded ED coatings in advancing the realm of metal corrosion detection and prevention. The meticulous synthesis and purification processes of the M-CDots, which were carried out using a solvothermal method, showcased the successful creation of luminescent nanoparticles with well-defined properties. The subsequent integration of M-CDots into ED coatings demonstrated a remarkable enhancement in corrosion resistance, as evidenced by extensive characterization techniques such as FTIR, SEM, EDX, EIS, and PL spectroscopy.

The implementation of a Design of Experiment using a Central Composite Design allowed for the systematic optimization of critical parameters, including CND concentration, temperature, and electrodeposition voltage. The ANOVA analysis of the DOE results provided valuable insights into the significance of temperature and voltage terms, guiding the formulation of an equation that describes the relationship between these factors and the response variables—adhesion strength and electrochemical impedance.

The synthesized M-CDots, when incorporated into ED coatings, exhibited superior adhesion strength and mechanical stability, surpassing those of reference coatings. Anti-corrosion investigations, including EIS, Tafel polarization curves, and salt spray tests, consistently demonstrated the efficacy of the M-CDots-infused coatings in inhibiting corrosion and prolonging the protection of metal substrates.

Notably, the response surface models and contour plots derived from the DOE data facilitated a deeper understanding of the intricate interplay between factors, guiding the formulation of optimal conditions for achieving maximum adhesion strength and impedance.

The incorporation of M-CDots fills voids in the coating, inhibits the diffusion of corrosive media, and forms a protective film, enhancing overall corrosion resistance. Their existence informs the start of coating failure under UV light before any sign is visible to the naked eye in daylight. This can be seen in fluorescent microscopy images.

The photoluminescence quenching of M-CDots in the presence of iron ions offers a promising avenue for early-stage corrosion detection. Unlike traditional methods relying on visual inspection or scheduled maintenance, this approach has the potential for real-time, non-destructive monitoring. While further research is needed to optimize the sensitivity and develop a practical sensor system, this study expands on existing work on M-CDot applications in corrosion detection by integrating the sensing functionality within the corrosion-resistant coating itself.

In summary, the groundbreaking fusion of nanotechnology and material science, as evidenced in this study, propels M-CDots-embedded ED coatings to the forefront of corrosion-resistant technologies. The synthesized M-CDots exhibit immense promise for industrial applications where corrosion resistance and adhesion strength are paramount, offering a paradigm shift in the ongoing battle against the pervasive threat of metal corrosion.
Masoume Kaviani Darani: Project administration. Shohre Rouhani: Supervision. Zahra Ranjbar: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
